statement.markdown: A linked list of length n is given such that each node contains an additional random pointer that can point to any node in the list or null. Create a deep copy of the original linked list so that the copy contains all the nodes and the random pointers of the original linked list. The given linked list is represented as a sequence of nodes where each node is represented as [val, random_index] where val is the value of the node and random_index is the index of the node to which the random pointer points or null if it does not point to any node. The original list is 1-indexed. The output should also be in the same format. For example, if the input is [[7,null],[13,0],[11,4],[10,2],[1,0]] then the output should also be in the same format. Note that the random_index is 0-indexed for the output but 1-indexed for the input.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains the number of nodes in the linked list. The next n lines contain two space-separated integers where the first integer is the value of the node and the second integer is the index of the node to which the random pointer points or -1 if it does not point to any node.
statement.outputFormat: The output should contain the number of nodes in the linked list. The next n lines should contain two space-separated integers where the first integer is the value of the node and the second integer is the index of the node to which the random pointer points or -1 if it does not point to any node.
statement.notes: The nodes in the linked list are 1-indexed. The random pointer can point to any node in the list or null.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the linked list
The value of each node in the linked list

examples.explanation: The first node with value 7 has a random pointer pointing to the node at index 0 which is the first node itself. The second node with value 13 has a random pointer pointing to the node at index 0 which is the first node. The third node with value 11 has a random pointer pointing to the node at index 4 which is the last node. The fourth node with value 10 has a random pointer pointing to the node at index 2 which is the third node. The fifth node with value 1 has a random pointer pointing to the node at index 0 which is the first node.

The first node with value 7 has a random pointer pointing to null. The second node with value 13 has a random pointer pointing to the node at index 0 which is the first node. The third node with value 11 has a random pointer pointing to null.

hints.text: To create a deep copy of the linked list with random pointers, we need to first create a copy of each node in the list. This can be done by iterating over the original list and creating a new node for each node in the original list.

After creating a copy of each node, we need to establish the next pointers between the nodes in the copy. This can be done by maintaining a dictionary that maps the original nodes to their corresponding copies, allowing us to correctly link the nodes in the copy.

Finally, we need to establish the random pointers in the copy. This can be done by iterating over the original list again, and for each node, we find its corresponding copy and the copy of the node that its random pointer points to, and then we set the random pointer of the copy to point to the correct copy of the node.

editorial.title: Clone Linked List with Random Pointer
editorial.intuition: The problem requires creating a deep copy of a linked list where each node has a random pointer to another node in the list. The brute force approach would be to traverse the list and for each node, find its random pointer by traversing the list again, resulting in a high time complexity.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force solution has an exponential time complexity due to the nested traversal of the linked list, making it inefficient for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: A better approach is to use a hash map to store the mapping between the original nodes and their copies, allowing for constant time lookup of the copied nodes.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves two passes through the linked list. In the first pass, a hash map is created to store the mapping between the original nodes and their copies. In the second pass, the random pointers are updated by looking up the copied nodes in the hash map.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that each node in the original list has a corresponding copy in the new list, and that the random pointers are correctly updated to point to the corresponding copied nodes.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm can be performed by manually tracing the execution of the algorithm on a small example input to verify its correctness.
commonMistakes: Not handling the case where the input list is empty
Not correctly updating the random pointers in the copied list
Using a brute force approach with nested traversal of the linked list
Not using a hash map to store the mapping between original nodes and their copies
Not performing a second pass through the linked list to update the random pointers
